subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Kennedy Space Speech and NASA
stimulus:
In 1958, President Dwight D. Eisenhower signed the National Aeronautics and Space Act, establishing the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) as an independent agency within the federal bureaucracy responsible for aeronautics, space research, and exploration.

On September 12, 1962, President John F. Kennedy addressed a stadium crowd of 40,000 people at Rice University. The speech, officially titled "We Choose to Go to the Moon," came just one year after the Soviet Union launched the first person into space and five years after the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first satellite into space. The purpose of the speech was to increase support for Kennedy's proposal to launch a mission to the Moon and explain why this program needed to be a high budget priority. The event was the lead story in The New York Times and was covered in newspapers around the country.

President Kennedy talked about scientific progress and how the space mission represented a major step forward for humankind: "We choose to go to the Moon in this decade . . . not because [it is] easy, but because [it is] hard. . . ."

Landing a person on the Moon represented an enormous challenge, and many doubted it could be done within a decade. But on July 20, 1969, NASA astronaut Neil Armstrong became the first person to step foot on the Moon's surface.

parts.prompt: Describe the informal power used by the president in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'bully pulpit,' 'power to persuade,' or 'going public' as the informal power. The response must describe what the power involves, not just name it.

scoring_points.description: Describe the informal power used by the president in the scenario.

required_elements: By giving this speech, President Kennedy used the power to persuade by directly communicating with the public and gain support for his agenda.
alternatives.correct_example: By giving this speech, President Kennedy used the power to persuade by directly communicating with the public and gain support for his agenda.

required_elements: The president used the bully pulpit when he gave his speech.
alternatives.correct_example: The president used the bully pulpit when he gave his speech.

parts.prompt: In the context of the scenario, explain how the power described in part A could have impacted Congressional oversight.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific effect on congressional oversight — either increasing or explaining the dynamic. Accept either direction (encouraging oversight or creating pressure that shapes oversight).

scoring_points.description: In the context of the scenario, explain how the power described in part A could have impacted Congressional oversight.

required_elements: The president’s speech could have encouraged voters to pressure Congress to oversee implementation of the president’s agenda, like holding a hearing on space exploration.
alternatives.correct_example: The president’s speech could have encouraged voters to pressure Congress to oversee implementation of the president’s agenda, like holding a hearing on space exploration.

required_elements: By bringing more attention to his agenda, the president’s speech could have encouraged opponents in Congress to call for greater oversight of NASA’s activities.
alternatives.correct_example: By bringing more attention to his agenda, the president’s speech could have encouraged opponents in Congress to call for greater oversight of NASA’s activities.

parts.prompt: Explain how NASA could have impacted the implementation of the president's agenda described in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must focus on NASA's bureaucratic powers (discretion, rulemaking) and how they affect implementation — not just that NASA 'would carry out' the mission.

scoring_points.description: Explain how NASA could have impacted the implementation of the president's agenda described in the scenario.

required_elements: By using its discretionary authority, NASA could have chosen how to best carry out the mission to the moon.
alternatives.correct_example: By using its discretionary authority, NASA could have chosen how to best carry out the mission to the moon.

required_elements: NASA could have used its rulemaking authority to create the regulations that the mission to the moon will follow.
alternatives.correct_example: NASA could have used its rulemaking authority to create the regulations that the mission to the moon will follow.

required_elements: The bureaucracy could have used its discretionary and rulemaking authority to address President Kennedy’s policy agenda.
alternatives.correct_example: The bureaucracy could have used its discretionary and rulemaking authority to address President Kennedy’s policy agenda.
